segments.integrated_caption: A man in a black jacket with red accents and a woman with long wavy brown hair walk smiling through a lab, passing a large, golden quantum computing machine. The woman exclaims, "So, this is it," and the man confirms, "This, this is a quantum computer," which the woman echoes. Upbeat electronic music begins as the scene transitions to Marques Brownlee's YouTube channel page, displaying 16.7M subscribers. A woman in a red top, speaking directly to the camera from a room with bookshelves, introduces the segment, stating she's seeing a "real-life quantum computer for the very first time with my friend and King of Tech YouTube, Marques Brownlee." She continues, gesturing expressively, explaining she's "finally understanding why quantum computers are such a big deal." The scene cuts to a news segment with two men in split-screen, one in New York and one in San Francisco, discussing quantum computing. On-screen text mentions 'NSA SPYING' and 'QUANTUM COMPUTING RESEARCH & APPLICATIONS' as multiple male voices rapidly question "What on earth is a quantum computer?" The discussion continues with terms like "bits, qubits," and the idea to "try all the codes at once." A male voice emphasizes the geopolitical stakes, stating, "Whichever nation first develops a practical quantum computer will have a tremendous advantage," as a text overlay appears: 'China Is Pulling Ahead in Global Quantum Race, New Study Suggest.' The term "Quantum supremacy" is heard. The woman in the red top, gesturing, admits, "I see a lot of news about quantum computers, and it's always extremely confusing." A male voice comments, "That was really well done, and I still need to re-hear it," followed by another male voice adding, "But also it's often wrong." The woman in the red top clarifies, "Quantum computers aren't supercomputers. They aren't bigger, faster versions of what we use now. There's something totally different," and explains this difference is "the key to understanding why they're such a big deal. Why governments around the world are battling it out to build them the fastest." The segment concludes with a close-up of the golden quantum machine, as the upbeat electronic music continues.

A woman's voice, later identified as the Woman in red top, immediately introduces the topic, stating, "Quantum computers are wild. She explains they are "kept at temperatures colder than space" and challenges the viewer's understanding, admitting, "At least, I didn't." After a bright white flash, the Woman in red top is seen speaking directly to the camera in a well-lit room with bookshelves and tech equipment, gesturing expressively as she explains that "Marquez and I both realized that there was this big, important technology that we really didn't understand." She announces their "adventure to figure out the truth about quantum computers," promising to show viewers "what we learn as we learn it" and how these "crazy computers might actually impact your life." Upbeat, driving electronic music begins as the video returns to the Quantum computing machine, now shown from various angles, including through a glass enclosure. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens inside a car, where a man in a black jacket with red accents and a woman with long wavy brown hair, initially in a red patterned top, are seen smiling and interacting with a dashboard camera. The man playfully remarks, "So it works. It's not broken. I swear I'm usually good with tech," while the woman explains, "Like the only function of this screen is to provide directions," both sharing a laugh. A third person is visible in the back seat holding a camera, indicating a production setup. The woman, now in a white ribbed top, asks, "You're getting this?" before announcing their destination: "So we're going to visit IBM's quantum computer." She expresses excitement, stating, "This is really exciting that they're letting us see this computer because it is, to my knowledge, the largest quantum computer that exists, which I say with confidence like I know what that means." The scene cuts to a news article headline about IBM unveiling its 435-qubit quantum processor, highlighted as the 'world's largest superconducting quantum processor.' This is followed by a dark, high-tech server room with illuminated racks, then a shot of a large, golden quantum computing machine with intricate wiring in a glass enclosure, with a man in a suit standing beside it. Back in the car, the man questions, "I was gonna say, was it biggest?" and elaborates on his difficulty picturing a quantum computer compared to a supercomputer. The woman then vividly describes her mental image as "the golden octopus," referencing a known press image, which the man confirms. She emphasizes the significance, noting, "in order to see that, they have to shut down the computer. So this is like took some real logistics on their part," to which the man agrees, calling it "a real deal." The final frames show the quantum machine again, reinforcing its central role, as the woman asks the man what he finds most interesting about quantum computing, and he begins to answer.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a stylized, isometric 3D animated world, a green landscape with mountains, trees, and a river against a solid blue background, as a female speaker introduces the concept of a "video game map" where progress depends on "doing math," accompanied by upbeat, whimsical background music. A 'PRESS START' button appears, followed by a 'CHOOSE YOUR MATH LEVEL' menu. The speaker explains that initially, "there were no computers," and tasks like geometry "took forever," requiring "pencil and paper" and "walking" across the map, which was "slow." The player selects 'NO COMPUTERS' (represented by a caveman character), and the scene transitions to a split-screen view of two low-poly characters (one in yellow, one in orange) walking through the landscape, embodying this slow, walking phase. They approach a wooden bridge where a horse appears with a '?!' above it, signaling a challenge. The speaker then describes the development of "early computers" that "unlocked new areas" and allowed for inputting "larger data sets," accompanied by the distinct whirring and clicking sounds of early computing machines. This is visually represented by black-and-white archival footage of large, room-sized machines with dials and operators using punch cards. Returning to the animated world, the landscape evolves with new structures like houses and a horse-drawn cart, and a 'NEW MATH UNLOCKED' notification appears as the characters cross the bridge, aligning with the speaker's statement that instead of "walking," we can now "ride horses" and "get farther, faster," underscored by horse galloping sounds. A mountain area marked 'AREA UNAVAILABLE' prompts a transition to a retro computer monitor displaying a modern desktop interface, as the speaker explains that "computers just kept getting better," enabling us to "solve more and more complex math problems," with sounds of keyboard typing and digital effects. The landscape then transforms into a futuristic city with skyscrapers, highways, and traffic lights, culminating in a split-screen view of cars navigating traffic, illustrating how the "virtual map has transformed" with "roads and cars," as car driving and traffic sounds fill the audio.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with an upbeat electronic track as Olivia Lanes, Quantum Researcher at IBM, and a group of visitors, including a man in a black jacket with red accents and a woman with long wavy brown hair, walk through a modern glass-enclosed walkway towards the IBM building, visible in the snowy, overcast background. The man in the black jacket expresses his intent, stating, "I go look for a quantum computer." Upon entering the facility, they are greeted by a staff member and proceed into a high-tech server room filled with illuminated racks, where a curious female voice, likely the woman with long wavy brown hair, asks, "What's going on here?" Olivia Lanes explains, "This is our Watson setup... where the Watson AI thing was displayed and it like competed on Jeopardy with like Ken Jennings," followed by a robotic voice clip of Watson saying, "What is shoo? You are right." Olivia then introduces the space as "Brent's lab," to which the man in the black jacket responds, "Great to meet you. Thanks for having us." As mechanical humming sounds fill the air, Olivia guides them further, saying, "So this is the lab." The camera focuses on a large, intricate quantum computing machine, 'BLUE FORS,' with golden components. The woman with long wavy brown hair asks, "That's a quantum computer?" Olivia clarifies, "Yeah. But actually, this is a dilution refrigerator. This whole thing is basically constructed to keep the quantum computer very, very cold. 15 millikelvin. It's the coldest thing basically in the universe. It's colder than outer space." The woman with long wavy brown hair confirms, "So inside that is 15 millikelvin," before a deep, resonant sci-fi whoosh sound plays. Olivia then invites them to interact, saying, "Let me show you the one that we can like play with." The man in the black jacket exclaims with awe, "This, this is a quantum computer!" as Olivia confirms, "This is a quantum computer."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a large, golden, multi-tiered quantum computing machine, showcasing its intricate network of wires and metallic components, enclosed in a glass case with a 'CAUTION delicate electronics' sign visible. A distinct electronic sound briefly introduces a subtle, ambient electronic hum that continues throughout the segment. Olivia Lanes, Quantum Researcher at IBM, wearing a black long-sleeve top, enthusiastically gestures toward the machine, explaining that "the quantum computer is a chip" within this larger structure, which "keeps the quantum computer working at insanely cold temperatures." A woman with long wavy brown hair, wearing a white ribbed top, asks with curiosity, "So this is all refrigerator technology?" Olivia confirms, detailing how "all of these cables... carry signals down through the fridge into the processor" where "they do their quantum stuff" before the information returns to "room temperature control electronics" to be understood by humans. A man in a dark suit and another man in a black jacket with red accents observe attentively in the high-tech laboratory setting. Olivia further elaborates on the difference between classical and quantum computers, noting that the core concept "comes down to bits," which are "made out of pieces of silicon." The scene transitions to a black screen with animated text graphics: first 'BITS' in bold green letters, then a purple octagon, followed by a yellow square with gold pins resembling a microchip, visually reinforcing Olivia's explanation. The warm, focused lighting on the machine emphasizes its technological sophistication.

She then offers, "if you're interested, we can close it back up." segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a stylized, isometric 3D animated world labeled 'MATHEMATICAL MAP,' depicting a partially explored green island with 'UNDISCOVERED' regions. A purple houseboat docks near a wooden pier on a small island with palm trees, under a bright blue sky, as a woman's voice (Olivia Lanes) introduces a 'virtual map' and 'boats floating around discovering new parts of this map,' with light, electronic background music playing. A text overlay reads 'SIMULATING NATURE,' a concept Olivia highlights as a new area of interest. The scene transitions to a real-world setting where a man in a black jacket with red accents thoughtfully observes a screen in a technical room, while Olivia Lanes, Quantum Researcher at IBM, gestures beside him. The man's voice asks, 'Simulation of nature seems really interesting. Why is nature one of the things?' Olivia's voice then explains that 'nature fundamentally is quantum, and it obeys quantum physics,' though 'we just don't normally see it.' The video cuts to a high-tech indoor farm with rows of green plants under artificial lighting, where a person in a white lab coat holds a test tube. A magnified view of a leaf reveals cellular structures, followed by a molecular diagram, as Olivia's voice continues, detailing how 'at a molecular level and like the atomic structure of molecules, everything has to follow the laws of quantum physics.' She further explains that 'it's really hard to calculate what a material or what an atom is going to do past a certain size,' and 'at a certain point, it just can't do it.' The scene shifts back to Olivia speaking animatedly, then to the man in the black jacket listening intently. A Tesla Supercharger station with a white car charging appears, followed by a lab where a gloved hand pours liquid into a beaker, overlaid with molecular structures, and a close-up of a DNA double helix. Olivia's voice connects these visuals, stating that 'if you're interested in like developing battery technology or creating new materials that have, you know, long chains of molecules, you're going to need a quantum computer.' The video then shows a large, golden quantum computing machine in a glass enclosure, with a 'CAUTION delicate electronics' sign. A woman in a red top points at the machine, while the man in the black jacket gestures toward it. Olivia's voice concludes, 'This is why people are so excited. Think of the new materials or medicines or molecules that we could make if we could better predict the way that nature behaves at a molecular scale.' The segment ends with the man and Olivia smiling and interacting in a brightly lit, modern technical space, as a whoosh sound effect marks a transition.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an upbeat, slightly futuristic electronic track as a man in a blue shirt with an ID badge greets a smiling woman with long wavy brown hair in a bright, modern technical room. The group, including a man in a black jacket with red accents and a woman in a red top, then walks through a glass-enclosed walkway overlooking a snowy landscape, their indistinct chatter and laughter audible, punctuated by an enthusiastic "Oh, yeah!" They arrive at a high-tech facility where the man in the black jacket gestures toward a large, golden quantum computing machine. The woman in the red top listens intently, then exclaims "Yeah, there you go!" and "Boom!" as the camera focuses on the multi-tiered machine with intricate wiring and a 'CAUTION delicate electronics' sign. Speaking with clear articulation and expressive hand gestures, the woman in the red top explains that "simulating nature is something that quantum computers could be amazing at," but warns that "we're also getting closer to another use for quantum computing, one that people and governments are more concerned about." The scene abruptly shifts to a news segment, where the electronic music fades and is replaced by news-style background music. A man in a dark suit from 'NEW YORK' and another in a dark blazer from 'SAN FRANCISCO' discuss quantum computing's implications for encryption. Their voices overlap, mentioning "completely new encryption algorithm," the ability to "break encryption," and the potential for a "fully functional quantum computer" to render "all forms of online encryption unreliable." Text overlays like 'ENCRYPTION' and headlines about Chinese researchers breaking encryption and the 'NSA BUILDING A QUANTUM COMPUTER DESIGNED TO BREAK ALL ENCRYPTION' flash across the screen. The news segment continues, prompting the woman in the red top to interject with a direct question, "Okay, now this is the one that I've been waiting to ask about. Okay. Why is everybody so obsessed with talking about quantum computers and encryption?" The video then shifts to a stylized animated world, showing a purple houseboat on a blue ocean approaching a desert island with a question mark, as a new female voice explains, "So, the first major algorithm that was developed for quantum computers is Shor's algorithm, named after Peter Shor, super nice guy. He invented an algorithm which..." The word 'ENCRYPTION' appears in a green box, followed by a news headline about Chinese researchers breaking encryption with quantum computers, and the segment concludes with a bold text overlay: 'That’s a problem', underscoring the threat to digital privacy.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Olivia Lanes, Quantum Researcher at IBM, a woman with short, light brown hair, wearing a black long-sleeve top, standing beside a large, golden, multi-tiered quantum computing machine in a dimly lit technical room. She gestures expressively as she explains its features to a woman with long wavy brown hair in a red patterned top, detailing in a clear, articulate voice how quantum computers can solve problems like finding prime factors for really large numbers, which is the basis for RSA encryption. Olivia emphasizes that while classical algorithms would take "billions of years" to crack such encryption, a quantum computer could do it "way more efficiently," in "hours to days." A close-up of the machine’s intricate components highlights its complex wiring and metallic structure, with a 'CAUTION delicate electronics' sign visible. The scene transitions to news article headlines like 'The World-Changing Race to Develop the Quantum Computer' and 'Why Quantum Computing Is Even More Dangerous Than Nuclear Weapons,' underscoring the global quantum race. A second female voice, likely the woman in the red top, then explains that this global race "now makes a ton of sense" because whoever gets a quantum computer to "actually do this" has a "big, obvious advantage." Olivia then adds that achieving this would require "probably like a million qubits or more." A press release snippet highlights IBM’s 433-qubit 'Osprey' processor. The second female voice clarifies that while we're not yet breaking all internet security, "Today, we're at 433 qubits," the record set by IBM, noting that "400 to a million qubits is a big difference." The video returns to the group, now including a man in a black jacket with red accents, as they engage in discussion beside the imposing machine, with the woman in the red top listening intently and Olivia continuing her explanations.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens in a dimly lit, high-tech technical room with dark walls and exposed industrial elements, where Olivia Lanes, a quantum researcher at IBM, with short, light brown hair and wearing a black long-sleeve top, stands beside a large, golden, multi-tiered quantum computing machine encased in glass. She gestures expressively as she explains in a clear, explanatory voice to two visitors, a man in a black zip-up jacket with red accents and a woman with long, wavy brown hair in a red patterned top, that quantum safe algorithms will be adopted sooner than they think, possibly within five years. The man in the black jacket, speaking with a distinct and engaged voice, asks if this means RSA goes away, to which Olivia confirms it will happen gradually, not all at once. The camera alternates between close-ups of Olivia’s animated face and wider shots showing the group engaged in conversation, accompanied by a consistent hum of ambient sound in the background. Olivia emphasizes their incredibly fast progress in solving problems that people couldn't even begin to crack two years ago, stating that nothing mathematically or physically prevents their advancements and engineers will always find a way. The man in the black jacket comments, "That's sick," and then elaborates on his analogy of the quantum machine as a "submarine" due to its different levels of quantum computing. The scene transitions to a bright blue animated graphic showing the quantum machine equated to a cartoon submarine, followed by text overlays listing 'Simulating Nature,' 'Encryption,' and '???' to highlight potential applications. Olivia interjects, agreeing with the analogy and adding that they are "just skimming the surface" and will continue to iterate and improve this technology every single year, constantly discovering new things. The group continues their discussion, with Olivia using hand gestures to emphasize her points, while the man and woman listen attentively, occasionally nodding or responding, all within the modern, industrial aesthetic of the IBM facility.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a vibrant, isometric 3D animated world, rendered in bright colors, featuring green terrain, mountains, trees, and a river against a blue background. A male voiceover explains that the boat was equivalent to other forms of transportation on their 'imaginary video game island,' but he liked adjusting to a submarine because 'there are levels to it,' describing it as a 'bottomless ocean' where 'the tech gets better' with 'no physical limit.' A horizontal menu labeled 'CHOOSE YOUR MATH LEVEL' appears, displaying four illustrated options, followed by a small, purple houseboat docked near a wooden pier with a green 'EXPLORE NEW MATH' button. The boat then floats away, and the scene shifts underwater, where a small submarine with a yellow dome and periscope surfaces, accompanied by a 'NEW MATH UNLOCKED' banner. The video then cuts to a real-world scene of two women in a technical room, one in a black top and the other in a red patterned top, standing beside a large, golden quantum computing machine with numerous wires and a 'CAUTION delicate electronics' sign. The woman in the black top explains, 'Here's how Olivia put the dream of how we'd actually use quantum computers.' The woman in the red top, identified as Olivia, then speaks, stating it's 'beautiful because like, of course, we all want to make the world a better place, we want to develop technology that's gonna enable us to do fundamentally different and better things.' She continues, 'part of the human experience is you just want to like learn what we are and like learn more about the world we live in, and the fact is, the world is quantum mechanical.' The scene transitions through a close-up of a monarch butterfly on a flower, a wide shot of a green field, a gloved hand pouring liquid from a test tube into a beaker with molecular structures overlaid, a vast solar panel farm, a crowd of people in a city street, and a view of Earth from space. A woman is then shown driving a car, looking out the window, with the text 'We had not arrived... we were lost' overlaid. The video cuts back to the woman in the red top (Olivia/Cleo Abram) speaking directly to the camera in a room with bookshelves and tech equipment, with the word 'HUGE*' in bold green text and '*if true' below it. She states, 'This episode was so much fun to make. Huge if True is a genuinely optimistic, journalistically rigorous show about finding ways to use technology to make the world better.' The credits roll, listing her as 'Producer + Writer: Cleo Abram' among others, as she continues to speak and gesture, encouraging viewers that 'the best thing that you can do to support us is to subscribe.' The video ends with the word 'SUBSCRIBE!' in bold green text appearing on screen.
